videos.title: Irã ataca novamente navios no Estreito de Ormuz
videos.description:
O Irã voltou a atacar navios petroleiros no Estreito de Ormuz e ameaçou bombardear empresas americanas no Oriente Médio. Já Israel anunciou que vai ocupar o sul do Líbano. 

#JornalDaGazeta #shorts
videos.transcript: Pesquisar transcrição Uma grande bola de fogo foi registrada em Isfaran, região central do Irã. A cidade é conhecida por abrigar instalações militares, inclusive nucleares. O confronto no país continua intenso. O governo iraniano disse que além de alvos militares, americanos e israelenses atingiram um local religioso e uma fábrica de remédios contra o câncer. Os Estados Unidos também sobrevoaram o Irã com dois bombardeiros B52 capazes de transportar armamentos nucleares. Em meio às bombas, não são apenas os humanos que sofrem. Esse gatinho foi resgatado dos escombros de um prédio pelo crescente vermelho. Na costa de Dubai, nos Emirados Árabes Unidos, um navio tanque foi atingido por um drone. De acordo com a agência Reuters, a embarcação carregava 2 milhões de barris de petróleo e tinha como destino a China. Apesar do fogo, nenhum tripulante ficou ferido e não houve derramamento de óleo. E o Irã prometeu ainda mirar em outros alvos. Hoje a Guarda Revolucionária afirmou que o país vai passar a atacar empresas americanas de tecnologia na região, caso outros líderes iranianos sejam mortos nas ofensivas contra o regime. Entre as 18 firmas mencionadas estão Google, Apple, Meta, Microsoft e Tesla. Em conversa com o presidente do Conselho Europeu, Antônio Costa, o presidente iraniano Mazud Pesquian, disse que o país tem a vontade necessária para terminar a guerra, mas quer garantias de que o conflito não volte a acontecer. Já no Líbano, um prédio próximo ao aeroporto de Beiruti foi atingido por início. Israel afirmou que lançou uma nova onda de ataques contra o país, mirando alvos do resbolar. Na região sul, que foi intensamente bombardeada nos últimos dias, veículos militares israelenses transitavam em meio aos escombros. O ministro da defesa de Israel disse que uma zona de segurança será instalada na área até o rio Litani, o que basicamente quer dizer que o sul do Líbano será ocupado por tropas do país vizinho. O primeiro-ministro canadense Mark Carney criticou a decisão de Israel. chamou isso de invasão ilegal, que viola a integridade e a soberania do Líbano.

videos.description:
A Guarda Revolucionária Islâmica do Irã (IRGC) afirmou ter atacado o porta-aviões USS Abraham Lincoln da Marinha dos EUA com mísseis de cruzeiro. Segundo a mídia iraniana, a IRGC disse que quatro mísseis Qader foram lançados contra o navio de guerra, que transporta caças avançados, incluindo F-35 e F/A-18. Teerã já havia alertado que os movimentos navais dos EUA na região estão sob constante vigilância e podem ser alvejados caso entrem no alcance dos mísseis. A alegação surge em meio a uma situação já volátil, com recentes ataques dos EUA supostamente atingindo infraestruturas importantes dentro do Irã, incluindo uma ponte em Karaj. Donald Trump também afirmou que os objetivos militares americanos no Irã estão próximos da conclusão, ao mesmo tempo em que alertou para novos ataques caso nenhum acordo seja alcançado.

videos.transcript: Pesquisar transcrição Something has fundamentally changed in the Middle East. They are striking each other directly with ballistic missiles, with drones, with the full weight of military systems that were built for exactly this kind of conflict and that are now being used. And the world that thought this was another manageable regional dispute is beginning to understand what it is actually watching. This is not a limited exchange that will deescalate through back channel diplomacy in a week. This is a military confrontation between two of the most capable and most determined military powers in the Middle East. and it is escalating. The trajectory of events that produced the massive missile attack on Tel Aviv did not begin recently. It began with a decision. Israel launched a military operation against a strategic facility inside Iranian territory. The stated justification was preemptive, a threat that was developing and that Israel calculated would become significantly harder to address the longer it was left unressed. In Israeli strategic doctrine, that calculation produces action. The operation was conducted. The facility was struck. For Israel, it was a necessary preemptive measure. For Iran, it was something categorically different. It was a direct attack on Iranian sovereignty, a violation of the most fundamental principle of statehood. And in Iranian military doctrine, responding to that kind of violation is not optional. It is obligatory. Failure to respond does not preserve stability. It destroys strategic credibility. It signals to adversaries that strikes on Iranian territory carry no cost. And a country that signals that loses its deterrence posture. Iran was not going to allow that signal to be sent. The decision to respond was made quickly. What took time was not the decision, but the scale. This was not going to be a token response. A few missiles fired to satisfy the requirement of having responded while keeping escalation contained. Iran prepared a large-scale military operation. A response that would leave no ambiguity about Iran's capabilities, Iran's intentions, and the cost of striking Iranian territory. When that response came, it came at scale. Ballistic missiles launched from Iranian territory. Long-range drones deployed almost simultaneously. Multiple systems operating in coordination toward a common set of objectives. The targets were not random. Tel Aviv was at the center of the target set. Israel's economic heart, its technological core. The city where Israel's national commercial and financial life is concentrated and from which its connections to the global economy flow. When the missiles began flying, the impact was immediate. Not just on the military systems that were activated to respond, on the people, on the three million people who live in the Tel Aviv metropolitan area and who heard the sirens and had seconds, not minutes, seconds to make decisions about where to go and how fast to get there. That is the reality of what a ballistic missile attack on a major civilian population center means. Not the abstract strategic language of escalation management and deterrent signaling. The practical human reality of 3 million people having seconds to respond to a warning that missiles are inbound. The civil protection infrastructure that Israel has built over decades was activated immediately. Every modern residential building in Israel is required to have a protected space, a reinforced room designed to provide shelter from blast and fragmentation. Public bomb shelters are distributed throughout urban areas. Emergency procedures have been drilled into the population through years of exercises and real activations. That infrastructure functions. It has saved lives in conflicts before this one and it saved lives in this one. But its function depends on time, on warning time, on the interval between the siren and the impact being long enough for people to reach the shelter that will protect them. And the fundamental challenge of ballistic missiles is that they compress that interval in ways that stretch the civil protection model to its limits. A missile traveling at hypersonic speed from Iranian territory to Tel Aviv gives the early warning system a very short window to detect, classify, and communicate the threat. By the time the siren sounds, the window remaining before impact is measured in seconds. People who are asleep, people who are outside, people who are in vehicles or in parts of buildings that are far from the protected space, all of them are working against a timeline that is shorter than the civil protection model was originally designed around. This is the civilian dimension of what happened over Tel Aviv, and it deserves to be understood clearly. Above the city, as civilians were moving towards shelter, Israel's air defense architecture came to life. Iron Dome is the system most familiar to people who have been following conflicts in this region. It was designed to intercept short-range rockets and mortar shells threatening populated areas. It has been deployed in large numbers across Israel and it has accumulated an operational record that demonstrates genuine capability against the specific threat categories it was built for. But Iron Dome is not the system that engages the ballistic missiles Iran is now firing at Israel. Those require the higher tiers. David's Sling handles the middle layer of the threat environment, engaging ballistic missiles at ranges and altitudes above what Iron Dome operates at. And the AOS system, particularly A3, handles the highest layer, engaging longrange ballistic missiles during their descent phase at high altitude before they enter the lower atmosphere. The three layers working together create a defensive architecture that covers the full range of the aerial threat environment. Radars tracking every inbound object. Fire control systems computing intercept solutions continuously. Interceptors launching to meet the threats. The night sky over Tel Aviv during the peak of the attack showed what that defense looks like in operation. Explosions overhead as interceptors found their targets. Light flashes as missiles were destroyed above the city. The sound of defense systems working to keep what was coming from reaching the ground. But defense systems are not perfect. They were not designed to be perfect. They were designed to be as effective as possible while operating within the physical and economic constraints that any military system faces. And in a mass attack where dozens of missiles and drones are inbound simultaneously across multiple vectors, the mathematical reality is that not every threat will be intercepted. Some missiles got through. Where they landed, buildings absorbed impacts. Streets that were empty because people were in shelters absorbed the blast and fragmentation that the missiles delivered. Emergency services deployed immediately. firefighters to the impact sites, medical teams to where they were needed, structural engineers to assess what could and could not be safely returned to. The response was organized and professional, but the damage was real. And the experience of living in a city where that can happen at any moment, where the next siren could sound while you are eating or sleeping or trying to maintain something resembling a normal life is an experience that accumulates in ways that individual strike reports do not fully capture. This conflict did not stay within the Israel Iran bilateral confrontation. Iran made a deliberate strategic choice to expand the target set. American military facilities in the Middle East were struck. Longrange drones were used. Missiles were launched at bases where American military personnel and equipment are located. The targeting within those bases focused on strategic facilities. air defense radar systems, military communications infrastructure, the elements of the American military presence in the region that provide the force multiplication and the situational awareness that make American military power in the theater more effective than it would be without them. Some facilities were damaged. The American military response was not to retreat or to reduce its presence. It was to increase combat readiness and to make clear that the attacks had not achieved the outcome of reducing American capability in the region. But the strategic message Iran was sending by striking American facilities is the one that matters more than the specific damage assessment. Iran was demonstrating that its definition of the conflict is not limited to the Iran Israel bilateral. It was demonstrating that other powers with military presence in the region are within the scope of what Iran is willing and able to target. That demonstration changes the calculus for every country with military assets in the Middle East, changes the risk assessment for every base and every installation, and changes the political environment for every government that has to explain to its population why its military personnel are in range of what Iran demonstrated it can deliver. The analysis of Iran's military capabilities that has followed these strikes has focused heavily on the missile arsenal. Iran possesses one of the largest collections of ballistic missiles of any country in the world. The range of that arsenal spans from short-range systems designed for use in the immediate Gulf region to longrange systems capable of reaching targets across the entire Middle East and beyond. The development of hypersonic missile technology represents the most significant recent advancement in that arsenal. Weapons that travel at speeds that compress the defensive response window to the point where interception becomes extraordinarily difficult even for systems that are operating at full effectiveness. The saturation attack concept is the operational doctrine that ties the arsenal together. It is not complicated in its logic, but it is extremely effective in its execution. The first phase deploys large numbers of relatively inexpensive drones and short-range systems. Their purpose is not primarily to destroy targets. Their purpose is to force the defensive system to engage them. Every drone that Iron Dome or David Sling intercepts consumes an interceptor. Every interceptor consumed reduces the inventory available for the next phase. As the defensive layers work through the saturation threat, their available interceptors decline. The magazine depth that existed at the start of the engagement is not the magazine depth that exists when the second phase arrives. Then the main strike package follows. Larger and more capable missiles with higher destructive potential, arriving against a defensive system that has been spending down its interceptor inventory throughout the first phase. The effectiveness of this doctrine depends on the size of the first phase being large enough to produce meaningful depletion of the defensive inventory before the second phase arrives. And at the scale Iran conducted the attack on Tel Aviv, that condition was met. The economic dimension of this conflict is the channel through which its consequences reach the daily lives of people in countries far from the Middle East. Global oil prices were already elevated before the large-scale strike on Tel Aviv. Military activity in and around the Persian Gulf creates uncertainty that the energy market prices immediately regardless of whether specific production or shipping infrastructure has been physically damaged. The straight of Hormuz remains the single point through which approximately 20% of globally traded oil must pass. Any realistic threat to transit through that choke point elevates energy prices worldwide. And the conflict that produced the missile attack on Tel Aviv is a conflict with the straight of Hormuz directly in its strategic geography. The disruption to global trade that flows from energy price spikes, from insurance market responses to elevated maritime risk, and from the supply chain adjustments that corporations make when they reassess their exposure to Middle East operational environments accumulates into economic consequences that affect inflation, that affect industrial production costs, and that affect the standard of living of ordinary people in countries that have no direct involvement in the conflict. This is how a conflict between Iran and Israel becomes something that everyone in the world has a stake in even if they are not anywhere near the Middle East. The question that military analysts and political observers are wrestling with is the one that has defined every major escalation in this region. Does this spiral further or does it find a ceiling? The mechanisms that have historically limited escalation, back channel communications, third party mediation, the mutual recognition by both sides that further escalation carries costs that neither wants to absorb are all present in this situation. Diplomacy is happening behind the scenes. Multiple governments are communicating with both sides. The economic pressure of energy market disruption is creating incentives for deescalation that operate independently of the military dynamics. But the factors pushing toward further escalation are also real. The domestic political environments on both sides create pressure to respond rather than to absorb. The military logic of each side's doctrine creates pressure to act rather than to wait. And the history of this conflict is a history of escalation consistently exceeding the level that observers assessed it would stop at. The world is watching. Every government with interests in the Middle East is watching. Every corporation with supply chains running through the region is watching. Every citizen in every country whose fuel and electricity prices are connected to the energy markets that this conflict is disrupting is watching. What happens next depends on decisions that are being made right now in Thran and Jerusalem and Washington and every other capital whose choices will shape what this conflict becomes. Those decisions will determine whether what is happening is a peak that deescalates toward a different kind of stability or the opening phase of something significantly larger. The missiles that hit Tel Aviv were not the end of the story. They are a chapter in a story whose conclusion has not yet been written.

videos.title: Irã lança ataque com mísseis de fragmentação contra Israel, Haifa é 'bombardeada' em vídeo; 'Dest...
videos.description:
O mais recente ataque com mísseis balísticos do Irã contra o norte de Israel marcou uma escalada perigosa, com relatos de uso de ogivas de munição de fragmentação em ataques nos arredores de Haifa. Múltiplos pontos de impacto causaram danos a estradas e veículos, enquanto um homem de 79 anos ficou ferido em Kiryat Ata devido a destroços da explosão. Avaliações israelenses indicam que os mísseis dispersaram submunições em uma ampla área, aumentando o nível de ameaça. O ataque ocorreu após uma pausa de oito horas antes de novos ataques atingirem o norte e o centro de Israel. Simultaneamente, Israel está considerando expandir suas operações terrestres para o sul do Líbano, o que reforça os temores de que o conflito esteja entrando em uma fase muito mais destrutiva e abrangente.

videos.title: ‘DIRECT HIT…’: Iran BLOWS UP Israel’s Top Drone Facility Near Tel Aviv, Netanyahu ‘In Tears’ | Watch
videos.description:
A powerful Iranian missile strike has reportedly destroyed a drone-linked factory in Petah Tikva, central Israel, marking a significant escalation in the conflict. Iran’s state-linked Nour News claimed the facility was completely devastated, with visuals showing burnt machinery, collapsed structures, and a large crater at the impact site. The target is believed to be tied to Israel’s drone technology, adding strategic weight to the strike. Reports suggest the missile penetrated Israel’s air defence systems, raising serious concerns. Nearby residential areas narrowly avoided disaster. Despite the destruction, no casualties have been reported, but tensions continue to surge.
